# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. 90’s-2000’s teenage white girl movies to take the pain away

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Cookie-cutter characters, simple but cute/engaging plots to take you out of reality a little bit. Like Mean Girls, The Parent Trap… etc.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clueless
2. “She’s the Man” is actually hilarious
3. Saved! (2004)
4. A Cinderella Story

Ella Enchanted

Josie and the Pussycats 

Miss Congeniality 

Raise Your Voice

Sydney White 

She’s the Man 

Charlie’s Angels + Charlie’s Angels: Full Throttle 

10 Things I Hate About You  

What a Girl Wants 


5. Now and Then, Sisterhood of the Traveling Pants 
6. Freeway

Heathers


7. Jawbreaker, sugar and spice, bring it on

Any of the Mary Kate & Ashley movies 😂😂
8. She's All That
9. Juno 

But I'm a Cheerleader

Buffy the Vampire Slayer 
10. Just got a tonne for my wife recently to help her through breastfeeding a newborn.

- Valley Girl (1983)
- She’s the Man (2006)
- Romy and Micheles High School Reunion (1997)
- She’s All That (1999)
- Pretty in Pink (1986)
- Get Over It (2001)
- Drop Dead Gorgeous (1999)
- Sixteen Candles (1984)
- Clueless (1995)
- Picture Perfect (1997)
- The Object of my Affection (1998)
- Bring it On (2000)
- Heartbreakers (2001)
- The Princess Diaries (2001)
- The Sweetest Thing (2002)
- Crossroads (2002)
- The Lizzie Maguire Movie (2003)
- Ella Enchanted (2004)
- A Cinderella Story (2004)
- Raise Your Voice (2004)
- The Perfect Man (2006)
- John Tucker Must Die (2006)
- Step Up (2006)
- A Good Year (2006)
- I love you Beth cooper (2009)
- Angus, Thongs and Perfect Snogging (2008)



## 2. Can anyone suggest a feel good romcom movie

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I've been feeling a bit down lately. I don’t want anything too heavy or mind-boggling...

just a movie with great visuals, good songs, and strong chemistry between the leads.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Crazy Stupid Love
2. 50 First Dates
  - Watched that recently. Such a cute movie. 

Do a double feature and watch The Wedding Singer. 
3. 10 things i hate about you. funny, romantic, and iconic
4. You’ve Got Mail
5. My Big Fat Greek Wedding or When Harry Met Sally 
6. Jerry Maguire is showing in theaters for the 30 year anniversary and it's my favorite romcom! I got my tickets to see it as soon as I heard - highly recommend you go see it! It's showing at select places next week only I believe 
7. Forgetting Sarah Marshall always helps me when I'm in a funk :) or the Princess Bride
8. Along Came Polly 
9. Overboard - Goldie Hawn

Just go with it - Jennifer Aniston

## 3. Any good movies like Inception or Interstellar?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Looking for something similar to Inception or Interstellar. I like movies that make you think or have a really good concept. Open to any suggestions as long as it’s interesting and not too slow.

Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I went down that rabbit hole recently and these are the ones i ended up watching

* Contact
* Apollo 13
* The Martian
* Sunshine
* Ad Astra
* Mission to Mars
* Europa Report
* 2001 and 2010
* Red Planet
* Solaris
* Arrival
* Moon
  - Came here to say Arrival
2. 12 Monkeys 

The Prestige 

The Machinist 
3. Predestination 
4. Arrival is what you’re looking for
5. Fight Club
6. Annihilation. It definitely has that same vibe of "on the edge of your seat, no idea what's gonna happen next" with just absolutely beautiful visuals. Personally, I think it's just as good(maybe better imo) as interstellar or inception and I love both those movies. 
7. project hail mary!!
8. Waking Life

1 Giant Leap

Jet Li’s Hero

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
9. Memento 

## 4. Movies similar to Shawn of the Dead and Hot Fuzz

**Score:** 16
**Body:** I love the british quick witted, sarcastic humor and the fast pace of these movies, I always have. Is there any other movies that have a similar feel/sense of humor. Heads up, I have already seen all of Edgar Wrights movies (Baby Driver is another one of my favorites)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Attack the Block if that counts, with Nick Frost
  - Awesome film, I don't see it recommended enough.
2. Lock, Stock, and 2 smoking barrels

Snatch

The Gentlemen 
3. Try four lions. Very funny
4. Black Sheep, it's a New Zealand comedy/horror. 
5. Spaced (TV show)
6. The Boat that Rocked (aka Pirate Radio)
7. Frequently Asked Questions About Time Travel 
8. I like the "it crowd" for a show

Waking Ned Devine
9. Not British, but the Evil Dead trilogy, especially the second one as well as Peter Jackson's early movies

In terms of TV shows rather than movies, Chris Morris' work is very funny. Brass Eye is his best work but Jam is good if you like dark/surreal humour (it is super dark though, not for the faint hearted)

Another great show is Darkplace

## 5. Effortlessly cool protagonist movies

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I just watched Ghost Dog: The Way of the Samurai and Ghost Dog is like the coolest protagonist ever. Itching for something else like this, Drive, Le Samourai, etc

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cool Hand Luke
2. Yojimbo 
  - Sanjuro also
3. Dredd
4. Jackie Brown, Rebel Ridge, One Battle, Training Day, Ronin, Die Hard, Top Gun (both), Den of Thieves, The Town
5. Brad Pitt in Fight Club (he's kinda the protagonist :) 
6. Taken, John Wick
7. Get Shorty
8. Elliott Gould in The Long Goodbye
9. Snake Plissken (Kurt Russell) in Escape from New York (1981)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What's your favorite scene that hinges on the other guy in the frame being able to hold their own with the A-list star?

**Score:** 4900

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/rhhqcmdndaug1.jpeg?width=399&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a2fe63790d3ff745f4b83d1e5be977a973c08af6
  - jon rahm was excellent here
2. Hailee Steinfeld shared a screen with Jeff Bridges, Matt Damon and Josh Brolin and absolutely held her own in True Grit. 


3. The scene in Uncle Buck where Macaulay Culkin is grilling John Candy about his life. Probably the best acting from a five year old ever. 
  - I just about tear up every time I hear about how good John Candy was to Macauley Culkin on set. Like basically a real uncle.
4. https://preview.redd.it/0xgwkyo4daug1.jpeg?width=1128&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=02bc97d2278e54e5cff239fc2a626521c0b00251


  - She can’t give out no information. 
5. How about Stephen Park as Mike Yanagita with Frances McDormand in Fargo?
6. Child actress Kirsten Dunst arguably steals scenes from both Brad Pitt and Tom Cruise in Interview
7. While largely resigned to meme status now, the meeting in Captain Phillips requires Barkhad Abdi (in his acting debut no less) to go toe to toe with Tom Hanks to set up the struggle for control between the two 'captains' of the movie.

It's a phenomenal scene between the two.

https://preview.redd.it/zgn4tstwdcug1.png?width=1438&format=png&auto=webp&s=73174931167ab017750d46021fa548d5ba67cd30

## 2. Anaconda 2025 is the epitome of what is wrong with the film industry. I want my 30 minutes back.

**Score:** 709
**Body:** I was doing laundry, and said, "hey, why not see this movie with a popular title that has popular actors in it". Already off to a bad start.

  
So the actors are constantly breaking out into laugher about punch lines and situations that couldn't be further from amusing. They are put into conflicts that are resolved in the very next take, so there's basically no suspense or satisfaction whenever the issue is resolved.



Pop music and famous actors. I suppose that's supposed to automatically make the movie 10000% better?



The movie jumps from a scene where the actors are making unfunny jokes, then suddenly you see a cheap drone shot of some landscape.. Which is actually laughable. Then it goes back to some random camera angles and more pop music. Then back to more unfunny jokes and the actors laughing, and basically repeats itself.



I stopped after 30 minutes. Simply could not waste my time watching the movie. What is even the point in such lazy screenwriting, cheap takes and over the top fake laugher at terrible, poorly written jokes?



It's a disaster.​​

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Rudd and Black don't discriminate.  They're both actually talented, but they will star in anything.  Even this. 
  - ![gif](giphy|xTiQyEWg7oABnbCPXa)

Edit: for the uninitiated [https://youtu.be/a8K6QUPmv8Q?si](https://youtu.be/a8K6QUPmv8Q?si)
    - Now, Tayne I can get in to. 
      - Nude. Tayne.
  - You can’t go in to anaconda 2025 expecting an award winning movie, it’s supposed to be bad. But the charm of bad movies for me is untalented people trying their hardest, not talented people half assing it. 
2. You were doing the laundry so you had already forfeited your time and cannot get it back.
3. Only somewhat funny part was when Jack Black was running from the snake with the boar attached to his head
  - Who’s idea was it to put the squirrel in his mouth. 
4. I’m not sure why you would expect anything different. 
5. I agree the movie had no depth, but plenty of people enjoyed it.  If the “film industry” has any purpose, it is to provide entertainment.  And that includes lowbrow content as well.  Plenty of people like Tyler Perry movies - are they a failure of the film industry as well?

## 3. OMG this movie is so much better than I expected!

**Score:** 607
**Body:** I just watched Godzilla Minus One for the first time and absolutely loved it! I expected a pretty surface level monster movie with some fun action, but it’s so much more than that. Every character that’s marginally involved in the story gets at least some depth and it’s a super emotional anti-war film about courage and the value of human life. Can’t wait for the new one coming out later this year!

What do you think of this movie?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They nailed it. Super impressive work without qualification. 
2. Absolutely belongs in the Pantheon of great monster movies. The special effects and music score are both outstanding, and it delivers meaningful character arcs/moments that almost all western monster movies fail to achieve.
3. It's a good movie, you should check out the B&W version. It's my preferred version. 
  - This is the canon version for me. I’ve seen both and the “minus color” version makes the special effects feel a bit less artificial, and the movie feel more period accurate. Either way, brilliant film
  - I’m old and I grew up watching Godzilla movies in the theater and they were always b&w. So I went to see Minus One in the theater and I was not expecting a b&w movie. It literally transported me back into time and I was an 8 year old kid watching my favorite monster. This movie I believe is now rated in the top 100 best modern movies ever made.
4. Usually im part of the "i only care about giant monsters in my giant monster movie" but the core story was so well done that i actually cheered when i saw them walk into the hospital at the end
5. One of the best Godzilla movies. 
6. That scene where Godzilla is chasing the boat is one the most terrifying monster moments ever in my opinion. They made his face look so scary. I definitely agree that the movie deserves a lot of praise
7. It's an absolute banger of a movie
8. I can't believe a Godzilla movie made me cry. Like WTF.

## 4. In Interstellar(2014), the moment they realize that "those aren't mountains, it's waves", the way they presented it, was the best scene in the movie, almost mystagogical. It managed to give me the awe feeling of the situation. And one of the best scenes in all space movies.

**Score:** 318

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Readily agree. Unbelievable cinematography, plus Hans Zimmer thundering organ music... that scene is wild !

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v-xh\_gq8sbk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v-xh_gq8sbk)

The way Cooper drops the bombshell is perfect.

Brand "... toward the mountains !"

Cooper, awestruck "Those aren't mountains... those are waves !"

And then: paniiiiiiic !!! Poor Doyle. Never quite understood why he didn't climbed back into the Ranger once Amelia was safely into the robot arms and the robot clearly was making it to the Ranger in time.  I presume he wanted to be sure Amelia was safely onboard *before* him. Bottom line: he valued her life more than his own. Very classy and brave. Except he died.
  - Every tick is a day back on earth which is a great subtext over the whole thing
2. Yes, but the Docking Scene was even better. 
3. I have no clue what mystagogical means, but im gonna take your word for it and agree with you
  - “ I’m gonna take your word for it” is the best possible response to new vocabulary.  
4. Those aren't pillows!

And the music, wow.
5. Still get annoyed with how slow and incompetently the whole crew, particularly Doyle reacts, and how Brand doesn't follow orders almost getting everyone killed
6. It was frankly not believable that earth scientists with all that data would have failed to predict that planet would have that environment or the horrendous effects of time dilation. It was a completely terrible choice for resettling humanity that there was no reason to even visit, the proximity to the black hole alone made that obvious 
  - Also it was really stupid the way Doyle died. He was just literally staring at the wave standing next to the ship.

It's a great movie with awesome music, but there's lots of small annoying things like that.
    - Lol yes as i just said to someone else "They could have spent months studying it from space and realising it had tidal waves that made it unlivable and that would have made more sense than the time investment they squandered by actually going down to the surface. In fact they should have been able to figure this out from earth before even taking off. Then Anne Hathaway gets the captain killed because she wants to recover the black box for data?? The data is useless, it's data on an unlivable planet! And the captain didn't need to die, why was he waiting outside the craft holding the door open for her? To be a gentleman?? If they wanted the black box they could have taken off, waited till the wave passed, and then gone down for it again. This entire scene was 200% dumb as fuck"

## 5. Remembering Max Von Sydow on what would’ve been his 97th birthday. Do you have a favorite character he played?

**Score:** 274
**Body:** I’ll start. Varden Reynard in Rush Hour 3

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ming the Merciless in Flash Gordon!
2. Brewmeister Smith 
  - Thank you...I could not for the life of me remember what movie the scenes in my head came from.

"Coo loo coo coo, coo coo coo coo!"
3. ![gif](giphy|p5Fiw6dp2cBeE)
4. This is a weird one: he has a small but wonderful role in Conan the Barbarian as King Osric. A ruler desperate to rescue his daughter from the villain of the film Thulsa Doom. He waxes poetic on his throne about gold and jewels losing all their value when compared to the love of a father for his child. It’s a great scene.
5. Judge Dredd
  - Dude didn’t age a day between Exorcist and Dredd 
6. Minority report, always remember him for that for some reason
7. the hitman in three days of the condor 
8. His name is forever linked to the Skyrim trailer for me. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Zone of Interest (2023) - Review

**Score:** 115
**Body:** Imagine the dream life that you can have in your 40s - living in the countryside with your beautiful wife and five children in a beautiful home, with a garden full of flowers, vegetables and fruits and a little pond in the middle, all maintained with passion by your wife; random days where you go to picnics with your family or just for a nice little swim in the nearby river; you have house helps, you eat good and you are passionately working on your dream project. And the biggest trouble that you face throughout this whole ordeal is that you just have to live a few months away from your family. 

That was the biggest trouble they went through. Not the active killing of thousands and millions, not the constant gunshots and screams in the background, not the smell of the rotting flesh filling their noses, even when you try to eat or sleep. Nothing. They had become habituated to it. We all become desensitised to a lot of things in our lives - we don't feel clothes on our skin, we don't see the nose in front of our eyes, we don't hear the clock ticking continuously in our rooms. But one thought has always troubled - how tough it must be to go through the killing of a human. But as history has told us, and we can actively witness, people become desensitised to the murders of humans. Even little children become desensitised. It's a horrific thought. We see the grandmother, who cannot spend a couple of nights there, because she has not been desensitised yet. But the rest of the family had no problem. They went on living their lives with pleasure.

For the protagonist, it is just a project. He is focused on getting a patent for his ring crematorium, maintaining the YIELD, and getting promoted and so on. But we are not talking about the production of random goods here, are we? It's a sickening idea. Random bones can be found flowing in the rivers. Human ashes can be used as a fertiliser for plants. The background screams can be avoided like noise. Even the Polish girl helping the hungry prisoners appeared like a fairy, one who would be present in fairy tales only and not in the reality in which the movie takes place. But alas, the movie takes place in our reality and is not some dark, twisted fantasy.

It was such a tough watch for me. I had to take breaks a couple of times. All the horrible things are happening in the background when we see a normal, happy family on the screen for most of the time. We don't even see any Jews. Yet this might be the most impactful Holocaust movie ever. The cinematography, the sound design and that harrowing piece of music in the end. I am still shivering. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it’s one of the most important films of the decade honestly. 

As far as Holocaust films are concerned most of them, including this, conjure feelings of anger, sadness, disgust, and frustration, but this one adds guilt to the mix. That final cleaning montage hits so hard. How much do we block out everyday?  Is it intentional or are we so desensitized that it becomes just another normalized habit? 

Mica Levi’s bookends on this film are some of the most powerful pieces I’ve heard in recent memory. I just watched this with a group of people the other day and we collectively sat in silence as that haunting final piece took over the room. Incredible stuff. 


2. One of the few mainstream films that seem actually prescient to the moment that the world is in. Absolute butchery And brutality done to the weakest while people live off their artificially created comforts while either literally doing the crimes or turning a blind eye. I genuinely don't know how you watch that film and don't see the comparison to the American/Israeli axis and how it's digested by the mainstream of their society. 

It captures what industrialized , genocidal violence looks like and how people can just carry on like it's all normal. It will stay prescient because it's how these imperial societies always behave. Be they the Japanese ,the British, the Americans, Israelis or any ither future brutes.
  - He clearly mentioned the same in his Oscar acceptance speech 
3. I saw it twice, and it was the second time that I grasped the full horror of the sounds: cries of suffering, gunshots in the background. It was as if the horror had taken its time before absorbing everything.
  - I don’t think there’s a single horror film out there that’s made me as uncomfortable as The Zone of Interest. The imagination coupled with that sound design, leaves a greater impact than any visual depiction ever could. 
  - Without wanting to sound facetious, this is a film that really needs to be heard in 5.1. The sound design is just incredible, and a major part of the experience. It's just always there, in the background of every scene. In having to ignore it all the time, the Hess family must have been driven absolutely mad.
    - My theater experience added something interesting to this, it was at a basement theater in NYC next to a busy subway station. You could constantly hear trains rumbling in the background. It added such an unexpected extra dimension
    - I agree. It also demonstrates a lesson in filmmaking. The language of cinema is complex.
4. I think the film really pulled off its message, more through the cinematography and especially the sound design than through plot or character. It was impactful but I can’t say I really enjoyed watching it. Artistically I think it would’ve worked better as a short, but I’m glad it was a feature so more people watched it since it’s a really important message.
5. Anyone who wasn’t blown away by this movie probably watched it at home and didn’t turn the volume up enough. No sane person would turn the volume up enough. Need to have seen in a cinema maybe where they assault you with it. 

## 2. Which is the best sex scene?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Which film has the best sex scene conveying emotions of the character instead of plain voyeurism from the audience? Did any sex scene actually felt part of the film instead of a gimmick? It could be of any language not restricted to hollywood or British films. Can be from any era or any genre. Do you guys like sex scenes in films or felt unwanted for such things? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Just off the top of my head: *Don't Look Now* (1973)

It feels like an intimate moment shared between two people who love one another, and also has the implication that it's the first time they've had sex since their daughter died.

I also love the editing between the act and them getting ready for dinner.
  - Yes. Beautiful. A similar editing method was done with the love scene in Out of Sight between George Clooney and Jennifer Lopez. 
2. *A History of Violence* (2005)*.* The first, playful and affectionate. The second, rough and charged. The contrast between the two mirrors their shifting emotional states and how hidden violence corrupts their marriage and seeps into domestic life. 
3. The sex scenes in Y Tu Mama Tambien are so important to the story being told and me as a Latino bi man. 

Each sex scene represents a different stage in the boy's development. 

Their initial sex with their girlfriends, messy and bad but on both ends because they're just teenagers. 

Then both of the boys first attempts at sex with Luisa. You can visually see how the boys go from overcome with excitement and lust to realizing they're in way over their heads and that the usual amateur methods aren't going to do it. For Luisa, they're futile attempts at keeping her youth and moments of desperation in the midst of her condition. 

Then, in the famous final threesome there's a growth from the boys to men that take things slower, listen to their partners, going with the moment and sensual feelings of their environment. It's much more tender and reflects the maturing the boys do. Maybe more maturing than even they realize as they do things they couldn't even imagine at the beginning of the movie.... 

For Luisa, it's her finally letting go. The sex isn't a method of covering up her failed relationship or her illness. It's living in the moment and accepting what may come for what is in front of her. It's knowing there's a mushroom cloud in the distance and dancing while you can. 

A perfect movie from start to finish. 

It's incredible.
4. The Terminator(1984) features a sex scene that not only feels natural but also essential to the plot.

Future human resistance leader and savior of the human race, John Connor will never exist if he doesn't send Kyle Reese to the past to have sex with his mother, Sarah.
  - Back in the pre-reddit, pre-facebook days, I spent some time on the imdb forums. There was a relatively friendly troll whose posts were almost entirely about why *Terminator* is better than *The Matrix*...with the exception of this sex scene, which is an affront against all decency and taints an otherwise perfect movie.


Funny stuff. Of course, it's also possible that this person was entirely sincere. 
5. Ugh I'm so sick of this "are sex scenes relevant to the plot" discourse. Action sequences, jokes, sick camera moves, it's ALL plain voyeurism, you're just voyeuring different things! Hitchcock understood this, his movies are all about voyeurism, but you're not voyeuring sex, you're voyeuring bad thoughts, sinister acts, shame and selfishness, and it's a BLAST! 

ALL FILM IS VOYEURISM. There is literally a rectangular window through which you are peeping into the private lives of other people! It is one of the core ontologies of the film experience! And sex is a part of human life, so it should also be a part of our art!

So yeah, let films be horny again please so we can stop having this awful mind-numbing puritanical sex-negative moralizing about how sex scenes are so unwanted or unnecessary or empty or distracting. If they make you uncomfortable, then idk, maybe figure that out for yourself, whether that means skipping them the way some people skip violence or the way I avoid horror films, or learning how to appreciate them as part of the rich tapestry of cinema.

Also, I nominate *Team America: World Police.*
6. There are a pair of sex scenes in *The Lives of Others* (*Das Leben der Anderen*) that showcase the social isolation of the protagonist, a Stasi officer, compared to the person he's monitoring, a potentially dissident playwright. The playwright is in a loving relationship with an actress; the Stasi operative hires a prostitute.
7. Lust, Caution has some sex scenes which are integral to the plot - they are mostly very suffocating and further make Tony Leung's character all the more unsettling as well as serve to show the lead's (Tang Wei) changing relationship with her sexuality.
8. A History of Violence, by David Cronenberg, features an amazing pair of sex scenes with Viggo Mortensen and Mara Bello. They are a married couple living a simple happy life in a small town. 

In the first scene, she dresses up as a cheerleader. The sex isn’t that explicit but it conveys the kind of playful, R rated sex that long term partners tend to enjoy. 

Then she finds out about his dark past when gangsters arrive at his coffee shop and claim they know him. When attacked, he skillfully dispatches them like a pro. 

The next sex scene is practically violent. Not rapey, but intense angry sex on the stairway. She’s both super-aroused by this new version of her husband and super pissed about the deception. 

It’s such a great film. 

## 3. In the mood for love is one of the best work of wkw

**Score:** 16
**Body:** The cinematography is actually insanelike every shot feels so intentional. The slow motion, the colors, the way the camera just lingers… it all adds to this heavy, almost suffocating feeling. Even the silence feels meaningful..

Also, I don’t see people talk about this film as much as Fallen Angels or Chungking Express, and honestly it deserves way more attention.

What I loved the most is how restrained everything is. They don’t say much, but you understand everything anyway. It’s more about what’s left unsaid.

And I really liked this one detailhow they try so hard not to become like their partners, but slowly end up mirroring them anyway. That part felt so real and kind of tragic. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It is easily his most praised work. In the sight and sound 2022 poll it was top 10 in both the directors and critics categories. Personally i enjoy chungking express more but in the mood for love is one of the greatest films ever made

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Sight_and_Sound_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time_2022
2. I'll look for it, thanks. In another of my film subs, I and another member just reviewed Fallen Angels; Chunking Express was name checked but I don't remember In The Mood For Love being mentioned.     
3. light SPOILERS

For me and my at-the-time gf, we both loved the drama of her costuming/dresses. Every single dress was just this amazing dramatic pop of color and personality. It points to her strength as an individual and to her ultimate decision toward the end. 

HIS ending with the wall and the greenery was just amazing and beautiful. It will stick with me for the rest of my life. The beauty of her costuming paired with his particular ending is worth an essay in my opinion. From what I remember, he wasn’t particularly colorful, just stoic. And for him to breathe color is just OOFUGHOOF. 

Thank you for posting OP. I didn’t have these specific thoughts prior and you gave me some space to think aloud on it. Thank you.
4. I think it's easily his best film. He's one the greats in general, but there's just some kind of magic in 'In the Mood for Love' that's hard to be put into words. I love to come back to it once in a while since it always seems to get better.
5. I always liked the odd choice with the camerawork, where every shot is being partially blocked by the something in the foreground. Some people say it's to create a feeling a claustrophobia, but I prefer to think it symbolizes the affair that's happening off screen. The symbolism of how we're not getting the complete picture of what's going on.
6. In the mood for love is definitely up there in Wong Kar Wai's filmography. Personally I am a bigger fan of 2046 (another less talked about Wong kar wai film) and Chungking express. 

In the mood for love is fairly talked about, not as much as Chungking express, and is lauded as Wong Kar Wai's best work in several lists. 

## 4. birdy 1984 is terrific

**Score:** 11
**Body:** wow this film blew me away an absolute banger which isn’t surprising seeing that alan parker as director and Nicolas cage and Matthew modine are the stars both giving great performances the ending is one of my favourites in all of cinema like i genuinely got fooled so bad aha a very bold choice to put a funny ending in this type of film but it definitely works i do think tho that Alan parkers best film is angel heart 1987

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes! I really need to rewatch it, it’s been years, but I remember really living it. 

In general, I think Alan Parker doesn’t get enough love in online movie discussions. He made some real gems in his career. 
  - he’s excellent it was so atmospheric definitely deserves a rewatch
2. The Peter Gabriel soundtrack is amazing. The acting is wonderful. I think Nicolas Cage got his wisdom teeth removed or something to understand the pain of the character? Something like that? It was a great film.

## 5. Mr. Arkadin: Welles’ Manic Energy

**Score:** 7
**Body:** There’s a point where I gave up on the plot, but there’s still something irresistible about the visual elements of this film. While watching some of Welles’s lesser know efforts, they all feature such an evocative visual style — and Mr. Arkadin is no different. Although, beyond the visual elements, it really was the editing that provided the film with such a manic pulse. It was almost Breathless-esque, with Welles not allowing any sequence to breathe and seemingly desiring to move through each sequence as rapidly as possible. 

I know that the film featured an extremely troubled production history, so I was wondering if the editing was intentional? Or is this just a consequence of the meddling of producers? 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t know as much as I should about the production of *Mr Arkadin*, but that rapid, staccato editing was definitely part of Welles’s later style. *The Trial, Chimes at Midnight,* and *F for Fake* all have it. It makes for an interesting contrast with *Citizen Kane*’s one-shot scenes and deep focus compositions, although *Kane* has the germ of his later style in some scenes.
